g.data-narration: Welcome to the Water Pollution module - an essential topic for understanding environmental engineering.
g.text: Understanding Sources, Impacts, and Control Measures

g.data-narration: Water pollution contaminates water bodies making them harmful to human health and aquatic life worldwide.
g.text: Contamination of water bodies (rivers, lakes, oceans, groundwater) making them
harmful to human health and aquatic life. 2 billion people use contaminated
water sources globally, causing millions of deaths annually from water-borne diseases.

g.data-narration: Point sources are identifiable discharge points like factories, while non-point sources are diffuse like agricultural runoff.
Physical pollutants include suspended solids and thermal waste. Chemical pollutants include heavy metals and organic compounds.
Biological pollutants are pathogens causing diseases. Nutrient pollutants cause eutrophication through excess nitrogen and phosphorus.
BOD measures oxygen needed to decompose organic matter. COD measures all organic matter chemically. DO is oxygen essential for aquatic life.
pH measures acidity (6.5-8.5 is ideal). TDS measures dissolved salts. Turbidity measures water clarity from suspended particles.
Water pollution causes water-borne diseases, disrupts aquatic ecosystems, damages agriculture, and creates economic burdens.
Eutrophication: excess nutrients cause algal blooms, algae die and decompose by bacteria that consume oxygen, creating dead zones.
Wastewater treatment has three stages: primary (physical), secondary (biological), and tertiary (chemical/advanced treatment).
The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act 1974 establishes legal standards and creates CPCB and SPCBs for enforcement.
g.text: Legal Framework - Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act, 1974

g.data-narration: BOD = Initial DO - Final DO. For this example: 8 mg/L - 2 mg/L = 6 mg/L, indicating moderate pollution.
g.text: Problem: Water sample with Initial DO = 8 mg/L, Final DO (after 5 days) = 2 mg/L

g.data-narration: Lake receives 50 kg/day phosphorus. Calculated concentration is 0.005 mg/L, below the 0.01 mg/L threshold for eutrophication.
g.text: Lake: 10 million m³ volume, receives 50 kg/day phosphorus, critical concentration = 0.01 mg/L
Result: Below threshold (0.005 < 0.01) - Regular monitoring needed

g.data-narration: Treatment efficiency formula: [(Inlet - Outlet) / Inlet] × 100%. With inlet BOD 200 and outlet 20, efficiency is 90%.
g.text: Inlet BOD: 200 mg/L → Outlet BOD: 20 mg/L

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: Water pollution from point and non-point sources. Important parameters BOD, COD, DO. Treatment has three stages. Act of 1974 governs India.
g.text: • Sources: Point (industrial, sewage) | Non-point (agricultural, urban runoff)
• Pollutants: Physical, Chemical (heavy metals), Biological (pathogens), Nutrients (N, P)
• Parameters: BOD (biodegradable), COD (all organic), DO (>5 mg/L healthy), pH (6.5-8.5)
• Legal: Water Act 1974, CPCB & SPCB enforcement
